The heavyweight boxer is famous for his incredible strength in the ring.
His aggressive fighting style often leaves opponents stunned and reeling.
Fans around the world eagerly awaited the first match between these two fighters.
In that initial bout, the boxer managed to land a total of 120 power punches.
This number was already impressive and set a high bar for future encounters.
The second match arrived with even more excitement and anticipation from the crowd.
During this second fight, the boxer increased his output of power punches significantly.
He improved his performance by exactly twenty-five percent compared to the first match.
To calculate this increase, we must multiply the original 120 punches by 0.25.
This calculation reveals that he landed thirty additional power punches in the second match.
Therefore, the total number of power punches in the second match was 150.
The third match became the climax of this thrilling series of professional bouts.
In this final encounter, the boxer continued to dominate with his powerful strikes.
He managed to land thirty more power punches than he did in the second match.
Since the second match had 150 punches, we simply add 30 to that number.
This means the boxer landed a total of 180 power punches in the third match.
Now we must sum up the punches from all three matches to find the grand total.
We start with the 120 punches from the very first match of the series.
Then we add the 150 punches that were landed during the second match.
Finally, we include the 180 punches from the intense third match.
Adding 120 and 150 together gives us a subtotal of 270 power punches.
When we add the 180 from the third match to that 270, we get 450.
The boxer landed a cumulative total of 450 power punches across all three matches.
This staggering number highlights his consistency and raw power throughout the series.
It is rare for a fighter to maintain such high output over multiple fights.
The progression from 120 to 150 to 180 shows a clear upward trend in his performance.
Each match was more physically demanding than the one before it for the opponent.
The crowd was likely in awe of the sheer volume of strikes delivered.
